title: Art Fund Museum of the Year Finalists Announced
summary: The Art Fund has revealed the five finalists for its Museum of the Year award, which includes a £120,000 prize, believed to be the world's largest museum prize. The nominees are Norwich Castle Museum and Art Gallery, Fitzwilliam Museum in Cambridge, The Box in Plymouth, The National Gallery in London, and V&A East Storehouse in London. Art Fund director Jenny Waldman praised the shortlisted sites for their extraordinary creativity and innovation, highlighting their efforts to open up world-leading collections and connect with communities through ambitious exhibitions and programs. Norwich Castle Museum recently reopened after a five-year redevelopment, now showcasing an authentic display of 12th-century life, while The Box, celebrating its fifth birthday, serves as a museum, gallery, and archive for Plymouth's history. The winner will be announced on June 25 at a ceremony at the Cutty Sark in London, with each of the four other finalists receiving £20,000. The prize is funded by Art Fund members who purchase a National Art Pass and other supporters. Last year's winner was Beamish, The Living Museum of the North. This announcement aims to inspire public engagement with museums.

background_read: The Art Fund Museum of the Year is an annual award established to recognize and support outstanding museums in the UK. It highlights institutions that demonstrate innovation, community engagement, and excellence in presenting collections. Museums play a crucial role in preserving cultural heritage and educating the public. This award not only provides financial support but also raises awareness about the importance of museums in society. Understanding this context helps appreciate why these five finalists were selected and how they contribute to cultural enrichment.
Article_Structure: Main Points: The article announces five finalists for the Art Fund Museum of the Year award, detailing their achievements and the prize structure. Purpose: To inform the public about the award finalists, celebrate museum innovation, and encourage museum visits. Evidence Evaluation: The article cites statements from Art Fund director Jenny Waldman and specific details about each museum, providing credible but brief evidence. Author Credibility: The BBC is a reputable news source, but the article lacks in-depth analysis or multiple sources. Methodology: Information is gathered from Art Fund announcements and basic museum descriptions, with no indication of independent research.

perspectives.description: The award serves to promote museums as vital cultural institutions that foster creativity and community connections.
